_meta.description: Age-group median net wealth as a ratio of overall median, used for age-adjusted percentile comparisons
_meta.methodology: For each age group, the ratio = (age-group median net wealth) / (overall median net wealth). Ratios >1 mean that age group is wealthier than average; <1 means less wealthy. Ratios are more stable than absolute values across time and allow clean age-adjustment without needing to update absolute dollar figures.

US.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
US.table: Table 2: Family net worth, by selected characteristics of families
US.citation: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2023). 'Changes in U.S. Family Finances from 2019 to 2022: Evidence from the Survey of Consumer Finances.' Federal Reserve Bulletin, Vol. 109, No. 4.
US.notes: Medians in 2022 USD: <35=$39K, 35-44=$135.6K, 45-54=$247.2K, 55-64=$364.5K, 65-74=$409.9K, 75+=$335.6K. Overall median=$192.7K. Triennial survey of ~6,000 US families with IRS-based oversampling of high-wealth households.
US.ratioDerivation: Direct: each age-group median / overall median $192,700

DE.survey: Household Finance and Consumption Survey (HFCS) Wave 4
DE.table: Table A3: Net wealth medians - breakdowns, by Age of Reference Person
DE.citation: European Central Bank (2023). 'The Household Finance and Consumption Survey: Results from the Fourth Wave.' ECB Statistics Paper Series, No. 46.
DE.notes: Medians in EUR thousands: 16-34=16.3, 35-44=65.7, 45-54=155.2, 55-64=230.3, 65-74=232.1, 75+=129.9. Overall median=106.7K (Table A1). Low homeownership rate (~50%) depresses young-adult medians.
DE.ratioDerivation: Direct: each age-group median / overall median €106,700

FR.notes: Medians in EUR thousands: 16-34=22.4, 35-44=84.0, 45-54=143.0, 55-64=191.2, 65-74=232.8, 75+=201.3. Overall median=125.7K. Relatively high homeownership and savings rates. Peak at 65-74 reflects inherited property.
FR.ratioDerivation: Direct: each age-group median / overall median €125,700

NL.notes: Medians in EUR thousands: 16-34=28.4, 35-44=94.8, 45-54=150.1, 55-64=204.3, 65-74=134.4, 75+=131.8. Overall median=105.6K. Wealth peaks at 55-64 then declines — high mortgage debt in younger years, pension drawdown in retirement.
NL.ratioDerivation: Direct: each age-group median / overall median €105,600

IT.survey: HFCS Wave 4 / Bank of Italy SHIW
IT.notes: Medians in EUR thousands: 16-34=95.6, 35-44=112.6, 45-54=180.0, 55-64=212.0, 65-74=168.0, 75+=125.4. Overall median=159.0K. Unusually high youth wealth (ratio 0.60) reflects high homeownership among young Italians, often family-assisted.
IT.ratioDerivation: Direct: each age-group median / overall median €159,000

GB.notes: WAS covers GB (not NI). Ratios estimated from published WAS age-band tables. UK lifecycle pattern is between US and continental Europe due to moderate homeownership (~65%).

euroAreaAverage.notes: Euro area medians in EUR thousands: 16-34=21.7, 35-44=88.4, 45-54=146.8, 55-64=195.2, 65-74=185.3, 75+=144.4. Overall median=123.5K. Used as the base pattern for HFCS countries without country-specific breakdowns.

scaledCountries.method: Countries without dedicated age-wealth surveys use ratios from the closest available survey. SCF-pattern countries use US ratios with minor adjustments for local homeownership and pension systems. HFCS-pattern countries without country-specific data use the euro area average pattern. All scaled ratios are documented as estimates.

scaledFrom.CA: SCF pattern (anglophone, similar lifecycle, similar homeownership rate)
scaledFrom.AU: SCF pattern (mandatory superannuation raises youth wealth slightly)
scaledFrom.NZ: SCF pattern (similar to AU without mandatory super)
scaledFrom.JP: Estimated from Bank of Japan FIES and lifecycle literature — high elderly savings
scaledFrom.KR: Korea Survey of Household Finances — moderate lifecycle effect
scaledFrom.SE: HFCS euro area pattern (Scandinavian welfare state, moderate lifecycle)
scaledFrom.NO: HFCS euro area pattern (Scandinavian, oil wealth flattens distribution)
scaledFrom.DK: HFCS euro area pattern (Scandinavian, high household debt in youth)
scaledFrom.ES: HFCS euro area pattern (country-specific HFCS data inconsistent, using EA average)
scaledFrom.AT: HFCS euro area pattern (similar to DE with low homeownership)
scaledFrom.BE: HFCS euro area pattern (high homeownership, closer to FR)
scaledFrom.PL: HFCS euro area pattern (Eastern European, flatter lifecycle)
scaledFrom.CZ: HFCS euro area pattern (Eastern European, moderate lifecycle)
scaledFrom.CN: China Household Finance Survey (CHFS) pattern — earlier peak, property-driven
scaledFrom.IN: All India Debt and Investment Survey (AIDIS) pattern — flatter lifecycle, multigenerational
scaledFrom.BR: IBGE POF pattern — moderate lifecycle in emerging economy
scaledFrom.ZA: Stats SA LCS / NIDS pattern — flat lifecycle, high inequality
scaledFrom.SG: Singapore HES pattern — mandatory CPF raises mid-career wealth

_meta.generatedBy: Verified survey data (US SCF 2022, HFCS Wave 4) + proportional scaling for non-survey countries

ratios._description: Age-group median as a ratio of overall median net wealth. Values >1 = wealthier than average, <1 = less wealthy. Directly measured countries: US (SCF), DE, FR, NL, IT (HFCS). Others are estimated from closest survey pattern.
